results.title: AutoMem: Automated Learning of Memory as a Cognitive Skill
results.content:
AUTOMEM introduces a framework enabling Large Language Models to automatically learn and optimize memory management as a cognitive skill, rather than a fixed architectural component. By iteratively refining memory structures and training a specialized memory component, the system significantly improves agent performance on long-horizon tasks, achieving 1.89x to 3.74x gains in progression rate and making open-weight models competitive with frontier proprietary systems. [...] Productivity: The "unproductive action rate" (steps where the agent was stuck or oscillating) dropped by 37% in Crafter and 65% in MiniHack.
 Efficiency: The rate of redundant memory writes (writing the same information multiple times) decreased by 83% in Crafter and 68% in MiniHack.
 Compression: Because the memory was better structured, the number of input tokens required per step actually shrank by 30% in Crafter and 25% in NetHack, despite the agents achieving more complex tasks.

One of the most notable emergent behaviors was a "consult-before-write" discipline. The trained Memory Specialist learned to search its existing files before attempting to record new information, reducing the memory-write-to-search ratio by over 50% across all environments. [...] The framework, called AUTOMEM, approaches memory management as an independently learned cognitive skill. Drawing from the concept of metamemory—the human ability to monitor and regulate one's own memory processes—this work treats memory not as a storage bin, but as a set of active behaviors that can be optimized. By promoting file-system operations (reading, writing, searching, and creating files) to first-class actions within an LLM's repertoire, the framework allows an agent to decide for itself what is worth recording and what needs to be retrieved.

results.title: Arxiv每日速递(2026-07-02) | LOUIS' BLOG
results.content:
3. 【2607.01224】AutoMem: Automated Learning of Memory as a Cognitive Skill

链接：

作者：Shengguang Wu,Hao Zhu,Yuhui Zhang,Xiaohan Wang,Serena Yeung-Levy

类目：Artificial Intelligence (cs.AI); Computation and Language (cs.CL); Multiagent Systems (cs.MA)

关键词：Memory, organize knowledge, science as metamemory, cognitive science, model

备注： Project Website: this https URL

 点击查看摘要 [...] Abstract:Memory expertise is a learned skill: knowing what to encode, when to retrieve, and how to organize knowledge--a capacity known in cognitive science as metamemory. We bring this perspective to LLMs by treating memory management as a trainable skill. We promote file-system operations to first-class memory actions alongside task actions, letting the model itself decide how to manage its memory. This memory skill improves along two axes: the structure that supports it (prompts, file schemas, action vocabulary), and the proficiency of the model exercising it. Both axes resist manual optimization: episodes in long-horizon tasks run for thousands of steps, and a single memory mistake can hide long before it surfaces, making human review of full trajectories impractical. We introduce [...] human review of full trajectories impractical. We introduce AutoMem, a framework that automates both axes. In the first loop, a strong LLM reviews complete agent trajectories and iteratively revises the memory structure that shapes how the agent interacts with its memory files. In the second loop, the agent's own good memory decisions are identified from many episodes and used as training signal to sharpen the model's memory proficiency directly. Across three procedurally generated long-horizon games (Crafter, MiniHack, and NetHack), optimizing memory alone--without modifying the model's task-action behavior--improved the base agent's performance ~2x-4x, bringing a 32B open-weight model competitive with frontier systems such as Claude Opus 4.5 and Gemini 3.1 Pro Thinking. Our results show

results.title: AutoMem: Automated Learning of Memory as a Cognitive Skill
results.content:
Hugging Face's logo

# AutoMem: Automated Learning of Memory as a Cognitive Skill

## Abstract

Memory management in large language models is treated as a trainable skill through a framework that automates both memory structure optimization and proficiency enhancement, leading to significant performance improvements in long-horizon tasks. [...] Memory expertise is a learned skill: knowing what to encode, when to retrieve, and how to organize knowledge--a capacity known in cognitive science as metamemory. We bring this perspective to LLMs by treating memory management as a trainable skill. We promote file-system operations to first-class memory actions alongside task actions, letting the model itself decide how to manage its memory. This memory skill improves along two axes: the structure that supports it (prompts, file schemas, action vocabulary), and the proficiency of the model exercising it. Both axes resist manual optimization: episodes in long-horizon tasks run for thousands of steps, and a single memory mistake can hide long before it surfaces, making human review of full trajectories impractical. We introduce AutoMem, a [...] `@librarian-bot recommend`

Treating memory management as a trainable skill rather than a static prompt engineering problem is the right move. Most 'long-term memory' implementations in agents are just naive RAG wrappers that fail because they don't know what's actually worth saving. Promoting file-system operations to first-class actions allows the model to actually curate its own state, which is how you solve the noise-to-signal problem in long-running sessions. I'm curious to see how this scales when the 'memory' grows beyond a few dozen files—at some point, the index itself becomes the bottleneck. This is a practical step toward agents that actually learn from their environment instead of just reacting to it.

###  2607.01224

### AutoMem: Automated Learning of Memory as a Cognitive Skill

Shengguang Wu , Hao Zhu , Yuhui Zhang , Xiaohan Wang , Serena Yeung-Levy [...] Memory expertise is a learned skill: knowing what to encode, when to retrieve, and how to organize knowledge--a capacity known in cognitive science as metamemory. We bring this perspective to LLMs by treating memory management as a trainable skill. We promote file-system operations to first-class memory actions alongside task actions, letting the model itself decide how to manage its memory. This memory skill improves along two axes: the structure that supports it (prompts, file schemas, action vocabulary), and the proficiency of the model exercising it. Both axes resist manual optimization: episodes in long-horizon tasks run for thousands of steps, and a single memory mistake can hide long before it surfaces, making human review of full trajectories impractical. We introduce AutoMem, a [...] The corpus shows that practice has matured most where the discipline says it matters: seventy percent of loops verify in the autonomous zone of the ladder and seventy-four percent name their terminal states, while automated triggering and durable memory remain comparatively underdeveloped. We close with the limits the practice must respect, including the verification burden, comprehension debt and the risk of cognitive surrender.
